text.hero.title: The Clearline Method.
text.hero.copy1: Most technology decisions go wrong before a single vendor is ever selected. They fail in the framing. It happens the moment a surface level problem is accepted at face-value, triggering an expensive solution built around entirely the wrong questions.
text.hero.copy2: The Clearline Method is named for the concept of the clearline in navigation: not the shortest path, but the one that accounts for the actual terrain.
text.principle.copy: Speed is a dangerous metric. Moving fast in the wrong direction only gets you to disaster sooner.
text.output.label: Output
text.stage1.title: Signal
text.stage1.subtitle: Separating noise from what actually matters.
text.stage1.copy: Before touching anything technical, Corbelle spends time understanding the business context, the decision pressure, and the political landscape. Who is pushing for this decision? What would success actually look like, for the business and for the executive personally?
text.stage1.output: A Signal Brief: a short, clear document that names the real problem, the decision that needs to be made, and the pressures shaping it.
text.stage2.title: Survey
text.stage2.subtitle: Understanding the landscape honestly.
text.stage2.copy: With the real problem named, Corbelle maps what is actually in front of us. Existing technology. Vendor proposals. Team capacity. A situational assessment that includes the human and political dimensions of the decision.
text.stage2.output: A Survey Report: a leadership-ready assessment of the current state, the options available, and what each option actually implies in terms of cost, risk, and complexity.
text.stage3.title: Sight
text.stage3.subtitle: Building a recommendation that holds.
text.stage3.copy: Using the Signal Brief and Survey Report, Corbelle develops a clear direction: a recommendation with a rationale that can be explained, defended, and owned. This stage also prepares the executive for the boardroom, the investment committee, the team meeting.
text.stage3.output: A Decision Document: the recommendation, the reasoning, the key assumptions, and a framework for making the case.
text.stage4.title: Stand
text.stage4.subtitle: Holding the decision through implementation.
text.stage4.copy: Decisions are tested once they leave the meeting. They are challenged, revisited, and eroded. This stage provides ongoing advisory support, not managing the work, but ensuring the original strategic intent is preserved as conditions shift and pressures mount.
text.stage4.output: Decision integrity. A confidential partner to think with as new information emerges.
text.practice.title: How the method flexes by engagement.
text.practice.card1.label: Tech Consultancy
text.practice.card1.copy: Engagements typically run through Stages 1–3, delivering a complete, leadership-ready recommendation over four to eight weeks.
text.practice.card2.label: Executive Leadership Practice
text.practice.card2.copy: Engagements run all four stages, with particular depth in Stage 1 and Stage 4.
